aliases: Seafood Nabe with Sushi and Tempura

english: Seafood Nabe with Sushi and Tempura

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Japanese seafood nabe set meal. 'Hot pot food product' (FOODON_03315671) is the closest defensible parent class. The set also includes sushi, tempura, and grilled fish components not captured by a single FoodOn term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Fish and seafood based dishes) is the closest defensible parent match for this mixed Japanese seafood set. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for nabe hotpot set meals.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for Japanese seafood hotpot set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One full Japanese seafood set meal comprising a nabe hotpot, sushi pieces, tempura, and grilled fish, weighing approximately 800 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Composite estimate for Japanese seafood hotpot set (fish and seafood dishes, sushi rice, tempura, grilled fish components)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for a complete Japanese seafood nabe set meal. Per-100g nutrient values were estimated by weighted-average composition of USDA FoodData Central entries for: seafood hotpot/fish stew components (FDC ID 174202 — mixed seafood), sushi (FDC ID 169238 — sushi with fish), tempura shrimp (FDC ID 175168), and grilled fish (FDC ID 175167 — baked/grilled mixed fish). The input record's per_100g values (energy 137.5 kcal, protein 10.63 g, fat 6.88 g, carbohydrate 9.38 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with cross-validation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data are not reliably available for this composite and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — sushi and mixed Japanese rice-based meals
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a complete Japanese seafood nabe set meal. GI estimated at 52 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sushi rice (GI ~55, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) and mixed Japanese set meals with seafood and vegetables. The tempura and nabe broth components contribute minimal carbohydrate relative to sushi rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate source. Weighted GI estimated at 52 reflecting the moderating effect of protein (10.63 g/100g), fat (6.88 g/100g), and dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g) on glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (75.04 g from 800 g × 9.38 g/100g) / 100 = 39.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 350 mg/100g; estimated 2800 mg per 800 g serving — well above the 2000 mg/day recommended limit for hypertensive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and dashi broth, avoid adding table soy sauce or pickled condiments, limit the tempura component, and consider sharing the set to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (9.38 g/100g; ~75 g per serving) from sushi rice; GI estimated at 52 (low-medium boundary); high protein (10.63 g/100g) moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit sushi rice portions and tempura batter to manage cumulative carbohydrate load, monitor post-meal blood glucose, and prioritise the nabe hotpot and grilled fish components which have a lower glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 350 mg/100g and cholesterol 43.75 mg/100g are elevated; however, seafood provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and saturated fat is relatively low at 1.5 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium and cholesterol exposure, prioritise the seafood hotpot and grilled fish components for their omega-3 content, and minimise the deep-fried tempura component which contributes additional saturated fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Seafood components (salmon, mackerel) provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 EPA/DHA; however, tempura generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and soy sauce contributes pro-inflammatory sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3-rich seafood and ginger components of the nabe, but should limit the tempura portion and request reduced soy sauce to minimise AGE and sodium-related inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Japanese Seafood Hotpot Set (Nabe), sold under the menu label '401D Nabe Seafood Set(D)', is a comprehensive Japanese set meal served in Singapore. It centres on a kaisen nabe — a seafood-laden Japanese hotpot simmered in a savoury dashi broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin — typically containing fish fillets, prawns, scallops, clams, tofu, napa cabbage, shiitake and enoki mushrooms, and nori seaweed. The set is accompanied by sushi (vinegared rice topped with raw fish or seafood), tempura (lightly battered and deep-fried seafood and vegetables), and grilled fish. The nabe component is warming, umami-rich, and lightly briny; sushi offers clean, delicate flavours with a slight tang from vinegared rice; tempura provides a crisp, light texture contrasting with the soft hotpot ingredients; and grilled fish delivers a smoky, savoury finish. Per 100 g, the set provides approximately 137.5 kcal, 10.63 g protein, 6.88 g fat (1.5 g saturated), 9.38 g carbohydrate, 1.25 g sugar, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 350 mg sodium. The 800 g full serving delivers approximately 1100 kcal. Clinically, the high sodium content (estimated ~2800 mg per serving) is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The moderate carbohydrate load from sushi rice (GI ~52) requires portion awareness for type 2 diabetes. The seafood components contribute beneficial omega-3 fatty acids relevant to cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. This dish is not suitable for shellfish or fish allergen sufferers, and the tempura component contains wheat gluten.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Japanese seafood hotpot set?
Is 401D Nabe Seafood Set good for diabetics?
Sodium content of Japanese nabe set meal
Can I eat kaisen nabe with high blood pressure?
What is in a Japanese seafood set meal?
Is Japanese seafood set meal high in cholesterol?
Japanese set meal for cardiovascular health

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for seafood hotpot, sushi, tempura, and grilled fish components. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact set meal. The input record's per_100g values are consistent with the USDA composite estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sushi rice data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the macronutrient profile is internally consistent and cross-validated against multiple USDA component entries.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Japanese seafood set meal are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026. The input record's data_source was listed as 'grabfood' with provenance 'ai_generated' and confidence 0.7; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component entries and retained where consistent. The original input source citation (HPB Singapore Foodo at foodo.sg) was listed as supporting 'menu_listing' only and does not provide nutrient data for this dish; it has not been used as a nutrient evidence source. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sushi rice entries (GI ~55) adjusted downward for the moderating effect of protein, fat, and fibre in the full set meal. Glycemic load per serving = 52 × (800 × 9.38/100) / 100 = 52 × 75.04 / 100 = 39.0. The dish is served in Singapore under a GrabFood menu listing; country_of_origin is Singapore for the commercial listing, with Japanese culinary origin. Halal status is unconfirmed as dashi may contain non-halal fish products and mirin contains alcohol. Iteration 3 fix: ontology.foodon_match_type corrected from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to satisfy G5 hard gate which requires match_type values from the set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
